text:
Recent epidemiological studies show that generalized anx-
iety disorder (GAD) is one of the most prevalent anxiety
disorders (Kessler et al., 1994). With the advent of the
DSM-IV (American Psychiatric Association, 1994), the
diagnosis of GAD replaced the overanxious disorder of
childhood, thus indicating a clear continuity between the
childhood disorder and adult GAD. Studies exploring
the age at onset of GAD have found that adult patients
often spontaneously report having been worriers all their
lives. In most patients, GAD develops gradually during
adolescence and reaches clinical signiﬁcance during young
adulthood (Léger et al., 1999; Rapee, 1991; Yonkers et al.,
1996). Even though GAD, together with speciﬁc pho-
bias, is the anxiety disorder that occurs most frequently
before the age of 18 (Scheibe and Albus, 1992), very few
studies have examined the characteristics of GAD among
children or adolescents.

Based on a model of excessive worry conceptualized
by Dugas and colleagues (1998), Laugesen and Dugas
(2000) studied the presence of four worry-related cog-
nitive processes in an adolescent sample. Their results
showed that intolerance of uncertainty, positive beliefs
about worry, negative problem orientation, and cogni-
tive avoidance were all signiﬁcantly correlated with the
tendency to worry among high school adolescents.
Furthermore, intolerance of uncertainty, positive beliefs
about worry, and negative problem orientation each made
a unique and signiﬁcant contribution to the prediction
of worry. The results of this study suggest that this model
of GAD originally developed to explain adult worry can
also be applied to adolescents.
The study of the efﬁcacy of psychosocial treatments
for anxiety among children and adolescents is still a new
area. To date, two randomized clinical trials have used
cognitive-behavioral interventions to target some anxi-
ety disorders in children and adolescents (Kendall, 1994;
Silverman et al., 1999). The aim of the present study was
thus to explore the possible application to adolescents of
a cognitive-behavioral treatment for GAD (see Ladouceur
et al., 2000).
METHOD
Participants

Seven adolescents who responded to an advertisement in a local
newspaper were included in this study. They were four girls (aged 17,

Cognitive-Behavioral Treatment of Generalized Anxiety
Disorder Among Adolescents: A Case Series
ELIANE LÉGER, M.PS., ROBERT LADOUCEUR, PH.D., MICHEL J. DUGAS, PH.D.,
AND MARK H. FREESTON, PH.D.
ABSTRACT
Seven adolescents received a cognitive-behavioral treatment targeting generalized anxiety disorder.The treatment con-
sisted of awareness training, worry interventions, and relapse prevention. The worry interventions targeted speciﬁcally
intolerance of uncertainty, beliefs about worry, problem solving, and cognitive avoidance. According to the Anxiety
Disorders Interview Schedule for DSM-IV diagnosis of generalized anxiety disorder, self-report questionnaire scores,
and time spent worrying every day, three adolescents showed clinically signiﬁcant change at post-test which was main-
tained at 6- and 12-month follow-up assessments. Minimal to moderate improvement was observed for other partici-
pants. Factors that may explain these differences are discussed. J. Am. Acad. Child Adolesc. Psychiatry, 2003, 42(3):327–330.
Key Words: worry, generalized anxiety disorder, adolescents.

J. AM. ACAD. CHILD ADOLESC. PSYCHIATRY, 42:3, MARCH 2003

17, 17, and 18 years) and three boys (aged 16, 18, and 18 years), and
all were French Canadian. The principal entry criteria were (1) age 14
to 18 and (2) primary diagnosis of GAD. All participants, and parents
for participants under 18, gave consent for this report to be published.
Measures

Diagnostic Assessment. The Anxiety Disorders Interview Schedule
for DSM-IV (ADIS-IV) (Brown et al., 1994) was used to diagnose
participants and evaluate treatment outcome. The pretreatment ADIS-
IV was conducted by the therapist. An independent assessor who was
not involved in pretreatment assessment or in treatment conducted
posttreatment and follow-up ADIS-IV interviews; this assessor was
the same for all participants. The assessors rated the severity of the
diagnosis on a 9-point scale ranging from 0 to 8. Conventionally, clin-
ician severity ratings of 4 through 8 represent diagnoses above the
clinical threshold.
Questionnaires. The Penn State Worry Questionnaire (Meyer et al.,
1990; translated by Ladouceur et al., 1992) consists of 16 items that
measure a trait-like tendency to worry. The Worry and Anxiety
Questionnaire (Dugas et al., 2001) is a 10-item questionnaire that
evaluates DSM-IV diagnostic criteria for GAD. Only the somatic
symptoms section (six items) is reported here. The Worry Domains
Questionnaire-Adolescent form (WDQ) (Tallis et al., 1992; adapted
for adolescents by Fournier et al., 1995) contains the original 30 items
and 15 additional items related to teenagers’ experience. Examples of
the covered themes are school, dates, and alcohol or drug consump-
tion. Intensity of worry for each item is rated on a Likert-type scale
ranging from 0 (not at all) to 4 (extremely). The WDQ yields a global
worry score that includes both the range and severity of worry. The
Beck Anxiety Inventory (Beck et al., 1988; translated by Freeston
et al., 1994) is a 21-item state anxiety scale that measures the inten-
sity of cognitive, affective, and somatic anxiety symptoms experienced
over the past 7 days. The Children’s Depression Inventory (Kovacs,
1983; translated by Saint-Laurent, 1990) is an age-adapted version of
the Beck Depression Inventory, consisting of 27 items covering the
principal symptoms of depression.
Daily Self-Monitoring. Daily self-monitoring was done during base-
line, treatment, and follow-up assessments. Time spent worrying each
day was compared to a personal maximum of worry for a single day
that had been established with the therapist at the beginning of the
baseline. The resulting proportion was recorded in a booklet at the
end of each day.
Treatment

A single therapist treated all participants. One-hour therapy sessions
were conducted weekly. The average treatment length for the six treat-
ment completers was 13.2 sessions. The treatment consisted of aware-
ness training, worry interventions, and relapse prevention. The worry
interventions targeted the four cognitive components of the Dugas
et al. (1998) model of GAD. First, participants identiﬁed their own
manifestations of intolerance of uncertainty in their everyday lives.
They then chose simple behaviors that they performed to increase their
tolerance of uncertainty (e.g., ask a teacher a question to get infor-
mation about a class subject, even if I am not sure what the teacher’s
reaction will be). Second, cognitive techniques were used to help par-
ticipants reevaluate their beliefs about worry, which primarily involved
the perceived usefulness of worrying. Third, problem solving training
was used to address worries about current problems. The therapist
taught participants the steps in the problem-solving process, with an
emphasis on the ﬁrst step, problem orientation. It was explained that
poor problem orientation often takes the form of seeing a problem as

a threat, which impedes the resolution of the problem. Participants
were invited to look at a problem as if it were a challenge by asking
questions such as “What can I learn, get, or even gain from this situ-
ation?” Fourth, cognitive exposure targeted the avoidance of the threat-
ening mental images often related to key worry topics about highly
remote events. Participants described a worrisome scenario, which was
recorded for exposure. Repeated exposure was then applied with a
Walkman tape recorder while using covert response prevention.
RESULTS AND DISCUSSION

Table 1 presents scores on the different measures at
each assessment time. The clinical signiﬁcance of change
was based on the evaluation of treatment response, fol-
lowing the lines of Ladouceur et al. (2000). For self-report
questionnaires and daily self-monitoring of worry, treat-
ment response was defined by a 20% change in pre-
treatment scores. For the GAD residual score on the
ADIS-IV, a score of 3 or less was considered to reflect
treatment response. For each participant, responder sta-
tus was then determined as follows: criteria reached on
0 to 1 measures = low, on 2 to 4 measures = moderate,
and on 5 to 7 measures = high. Table 2 presents the num-
ber of participants in each category of responder status
at each assessment time.
Based on the ADIS-IV evaluation of GAD diagnostic cri-
teria, self-report questionnaires, and daily self-monitoring
of worry, three participants (participants 1, 4, and 7)
showed a large decrease in GAD symptoms which was
maintained at 6- and 12-month follow-up assessments.
Participants 2 and 5 showed a moderate decrease in GAD
symptoms, and the improvement attained was partly
maintained at both follow-up assessments. Participant 3
stayed unchanged at post-test and failed to complete the
follow-up assessments, and participant 6 dropped out
after the ﬁfth treatment session.
The small number of participants and the fact that one
of them dropped out during treatment are important lim-
itations of this study. The type of design used is also a
methodological limitation because it is not possible to draw
ﬁrm conclusions from a series of case observations. Despite
these limitations, this case series yielded some interesting
observations. First, it seems that it is possible to address
GAD among adolescents with interventions derived from
a speciﬁc conceptualization of excessive worry. Considering
the three assessment modalities, it seems likely that par-
ticipants 1, 4, and 7 truly beneﬁted from the treatment
program. The worry interventions seemed to be very well
understood by these adolescents and were applied through
homework to their daily lives. Conversely, the low treat-

ment response of participants 2, 3, and 5 might have been
due to the psychoeducational style of the therapy which
supplied a certain amount of new information and required
the use of notebooks and homework. For example, par-
ticipant 2 was going to college but was experiencing seri-
ous concentration difficulties during her classes and
procrastination problems with her homework. Participant
3 was registered part-time at college, but was not regularly
attending his classes, whereas participant 5 had not ﬁn-
ished high school and was encountering problems with
obtaining his professional diploma. It is possible that dif-
ﬁculties with the school educational system experienced
by these older adolescents had a negative impact on ther-
apy with similar structural features. Even though the cog-
nitive-behavioral approach used in this study involved a
simple and adapted vocabulary to explain treatment, it
may be that the format is less appealing to adolescents who
do not enjoy school. The way the worry interventions are
presented might be further adapted to more closely ﬁt with
the age and maturity of the adolescent undertaking treat-
ment of GAD. One of the challenges of future research is
to use a controlled group design while keeping the treat-
ment sufﬁciently individualized for each adolescent.
